rationale: Battery EOL transfer is performed under label-inconsistent settings.
There is a pronounced chemistry-driven domain shift: multi-family NMC/HE5050 sources → high-voltage 5Vspinel target.
Input evidence indicates short early-life windows (32 steps) with multi-channel electrochemical features.

A compact 1-D CNN is appropriate for local degradation signatures, while self-attention is enabled to capture cross-cycle and cross-channel interactions that may not be well modeled by strictly local convolutions.
Target class distribution is highly skewed (few samples in higher-lifetime classes), which increases risk of brittle decision boundaries.
SNGP is enabled to improve calibration and uncertainty awareness under shift, reducing overconfident extrapolation when target support is sparse.

OpenMax and explicit unknown heads are disabled to avoid unstable tail-fitting behavior when target data are limited and class support is uneven; uncertainty is handled primarily through SNGP in this configuration.
Moderate bottleneck (256) preserves transfer capacity while limiting over-parameterization for early-life data.
Dropout (0.30) and warmup (10 epochs) are selected to stabilize adaptation under chemistry mismatch and prevent early overfitting.
History access indicates SNGP-based heads and conservative optimization settings are more reliable in prior cross-chemistry comparisons, motivating the above guarded configuration.
